trajectory_summary: This call started at maximum hostility and ended with a booked appointment — that trajectory is a genuine rep achievement. Meg kept her composure across 21 minutes of sustained skepticism, made real-time operational concessions that de-escalated at each friction point, and humanized the closer transition effectively. The appointment, however, carries two structural vulnerabilities that elevate no-show and disqualification risk: co-title holder Andy was identified at 01:29 and explicitly excluded by Ashley at 11:16 without Meg applying the spouse-inclusion protocol, and the appointment is conditioned on Meg's personal presence on the call (21:13) — a commitment the closer Sam may not be able to honor and that wasn't built into the button-up.
shape: mixed — energy built across the call but structural gaps at two key protocol moments create appointment fragility that the trajectory alone doesn't show
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag 'booked / qualified' overstates stability. More precisely: appointment_booked=true; appointment_held risk elevated due to two structural vulnerabilities: co-title holder Andy excluded (protocols.spouse_decision violation) and appointment conditioned on rep presence at 21:13. The closer must resolve both on contact or the sit is fragile.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: spouse-protocol discipline — proactive inclusion at identification, not reactive recovery at exclusion
primary_coaching_focus.why: Meg identified a co-title holder at 01:29 and filed it as a data point. Eleven minutes later, when Ashley excluded Andy, the protocol window had closed and Meg had no leverage to reopen it without risking the booking. The schema is explicit: co-title-holder identification is the trigger for proactive inclusion framing, not the spouse-exclusion attempt. Every appointment Meg books without applying this protocol at the identification moment is structurally fragile. This call happened to survive — the next one may not.

key_moments.stewart_read: Meg learns Andy is on the title and moves on in one beat. This is the earliest and cleanest point to apply spouse-inclusion protocol — not as a future objection to overcome, but as a natural qualifier: 'Great — and would Andy typically be part of a conversation like this? We'd love to make sure he's on the call too.' At 01:29 the call is warm enough that this would land as thoroughness, not pressure. Instead, Meg banks 'Andy is on the title' as a data point and continues. This is the original failure; the 11:16 moment is the consequence.

key_moments.quote: No. No. No. No. It's just me. I'll make sure you're out of our texting queue. All of that, like, I've already got it marked and taken care of. It will just be me from my personal line.
key_moments.stewart_read: Meg's personal-line commitment is effective trust-building in the moment — it's a real human substituting herself for an automated system, which is exactly the micro-trade pattern Ashley needed. The problem is the mechanism: giving out a personal number for bill follow-up is the anti-pattern the schema explicitly flags. This call is a live demonstration of why the automated bill-capture capability is worth building — Meg had to expose her personal number to make Ashley feel safe enough to share the bill photo. The trade worked; the infrastructure behind it creates floor-wide privacy and scale exposure.

key_moments.quote: We do everything in house here at ION. Right? So we're not hiring people to go do the install, so we don't have to worry about, like, paying people in big lump sums to do things like that. Because everyone's a w two employee.
key_moments.stewart_read: Meg explains Ion's W2 staffing model to justify the no-upfront-cost claim. This is scope creep — the setter's job is to create curiosity and book the appointment, not to defend Ion's business model. The W2 explanation doesn't actually answer Ashley's fear ('how do you make money if you're not charging for install?'); it sidesteps it while using operational detail that belongs in the closer's conversation. Reactive read: Meg got pulled into justifying Ion's economics because she didn't have the flip available. Strategic read: Meg was buying credibility points with operational transparency. The reactive read holds — this information created no emotional movement and burned 30 seconds without resolving anything.

key_moments.quote: But like I said, I don't wanna spend any more money. So you say there's no left front cost, but what are the benefits cost?
key_moments.stewart_read: Ashley is asking the right question — 'what's this actually going to cost me?' — and Meg responds with a feature repeat: 'it's just that monthly payment that replaces Colorado Springs.' This is the second clean flip opportunity on the hidden-costs concern. The flip available here: Ashley has already said she's paying $262/month to Colorado Springs. The question 'what are the benefits cost?' is the exact setup for 'the only cost is a payment that's lower than your current $262 — you're already paying this money, just to the wrong company.' Meg had the bill number. She never used it as leverage. Per schema numeric_discipline, the $262 is grounded in the transcript and is fair game.
